serving.natural_language_portion: One standard plate serving of pork belly papaya salad is approximately 250 g, comprising grilled pork belly slices and shredded green papaya salad with dressing.

nutrient_source.title: Pork Belly Papaya Salad — Singapore HPB Foodo Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for Pork Belly Papaya Salad (cr_id G00010617). All per-100g values preserved from the canonical HPB record. Saturated fat estimated at approximately 13 g/100g based on USDA FoodData Central pork belly reference (USDA SR Legacy item 10006) proportional to total fat of 44.54 g/100g; this is documented as an adapted value.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Pork Belly Papaya Salad. GI estimated at 52 based on the analogous food 'green papaya / mixed vegetable salad with dressing' from the University of Sydney GI Database. The dish is predominantly fat and protein (pork belly) with a modest carbohydrate contribution from green papaya (5.9 g/100g). The low-GI estimate reflects the high fat content which slows gastric emptying and the low net carbohydrate load. GL per serving calculated as: GI (52) × carbohydrate per serving (14.75 g for 250 g serving) / 100 = 7.67, rounded to 7.6.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 810 mg/100g — substantially exceeds the 600 mg/100g upper threshold; contributed by fish sauce, dried shrimp, and pork belly.
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should avoid or strictly limit this dish; a 250 g serving delivers approximately 2025 mg sodium, representing over 85% of the recommended daily sodium limit of 2300 mg, and the high saturated fat content further elevates cardiovascular risk.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Extremely high fat density (44.54 g/100g) with zero dietary fibre impairs insulin sensitivity; saturated fat from pork belly worsens peripheral insulin resistance.
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with type 2 diabetes should avoid this dish in its standard form; the very high saturated fat content and caloric density (430 kcal/100g) with no dietary fibre promote insulin resistance, and if consumed, portion size should be strictly limited to no more than 100 g with a high-fibre accompaniment.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High saturated fat (~13 g/100g), high sodium (810 mg/100g), and high total fat (44.54 g/100g) collectively elevate LDL cholesterol and blood pressure.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: This dish is not suitable for individuals with cardiovascular disease; a single 250 g serving provides approximately 32.5 g saturated fat and 2025 mg sodium, both of which substantially exceed recommended daily limits and contribute to atherosclerotic risk.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Pork belly is a red meat high in arachidonic acid (pro-inflammatory omega-6); chilli is a nightshade ingredient; sodium at 810 mg/100g worsens tissue oedema.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid this dish; the combination of red meat (arachidonic acid), nightshade ingredient (chilli), high saturated fat, and high sodium triggers multiple RA inflammatory pathways and may exacerbate joint flares.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Pork Belly Papaya Salad (ဝက်ကင် Papaya ထောင်း) is a Singaporean dish with Burmese culinary influence, combining grilled or roasted pork belly slices with shredded green papaya dressed in a pungent, tangy sauce of fish sauce, lime juice, dried shrimp, chilli, garlic, and palm sugar. The dish is served as a salad, offering a contrast between the rich, fatty, slightly charred pork belly and the crisp, refreshing raw green papaya. The flavour profile is simultaneously savoury, sour, spicy, and mildly sweet. Nutritionally, the dish is calorie-dense at 430 kcal per 100 g, driven almost entirely by the very high fat content of 44.54 g/100g, of which approximately 13 g is saturated fat. Protein is unusually low at 1.32 g/100g relative to the fat content, reflecting the high proportion of fatty tissue in pork belly. Carbohydrates are modest at 5.9 g/100g, primarily from the papaya and palm sugar. Sodium is very high at 810 mg/100g due to fish sauce and dried shrimp. Dietary fibre is negligible at 0 g/100g. The dish is not suitable for individuals managing hypertension, type 2 diabetes, cardiovascular disease, or rheumatoid arthritis. It is commonly consumed as a lunch or dinner dish in Singapore and is associated with the Burmese community. The Burmese name ဝက်ကင် Papaya ထောင်း translates literally as 'grilled pork papaya pounded salad', referencing the traditional mortar-and-pestle preparation technique used for the papaya component.

confidence_reason: Core nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo canonical database entry (cr_id G00010617, confidence 0.95). Saturated fat adapted from USDA SR Legacy pork belly reference. GI is an analogous estimate documented with full basis. All mandatory multilingual names are present.
notes: 1. dietary_fibre_g is confirmed zero per HPB source for this dish preparation. 2. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractions, vitamin panel, and moisture data for this specific Singaporean pork belly papaya salad preparation are not available in the HPB Foodo database or USDA FoodData Central as of 2026. 3. saturated_fat_g value of 13.0 g/100g is an adapted estimate derived from USDA SR Legacy item 10006 (pork belly, raw) proportional fatty acid profile applied to the HPB total fat value of 44.54 g/100g; this is documented as an adapted value. 4. GI of 52 is an analogous estimate based on green papaya mixed salad with dressing from the University of Sydney GI Database; no direct GI measurement for this dish exists in approved sources. 5. The dish contains non-halal ingredients (pork belly) and is therefore not suitable for Muslim consumers. 6. Allergens include fish (fish sauce) and crustaceans (dried shrimp).
